title: Lathyrus lenticula
intro: Lathyrus lenticula, commonly known as the blue vetch or narrow-leaved peashooter, is a species of flowering plant in the pea family (Fabaceae). The plant has been found to have several health benefits, including its potential to reduce inflammation and improve cardiovascular health, due to its high levels of flavonoids and saponins. Bioactive constituents of Lathyrus lenticula include isoflavones, such as genistein and daidzein, which have been shown to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. In traditional herbal medicine, the plant is often used to make teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat a range of conditions, including menopausal symptoms and digestive issues.
taxonomy: Lathyrus lenticula is a species of flowering plant that belongs to the family Fabaceae, commonly known as legumes. This classification is based on the plant's characteristics, such as its pea-like flowers and fruit. The genus Lathyrus is a group of flowering plants that are commonly known as peavines. The Fabaceae family is one of the largest and most widespread groups of flowering plants, with over 19,000 known species. Lathyrus lenticula is a specific species within this family, known for its unique characteristics and growth habits.
uses: Lathyrus lenticula is a type of pea or vetch that has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. Its seeds contain a compound called neurolathyrine, which has been found to have potential anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. In some cultures, the plant has been used to treat conditions such as fever, cough, and respiratory issues. Additionally, its flowers and pods have been used in traditional medicine to treat digestive problems and as a diuretic. Further research is needed to fully understand the medicinal uses and potential benefits of Lathyrus lenticula.

side_effects: Lathyrus lenticula, also known as the purple peavine or purple vetchling, contains a toxin called ODAP (oxalyldiaminopropionic acid), which can cause severe health issues if consumed in large quantities. Prolonged consumption of ODAP can lead to neurolathyrism, a condition characterized by muscle spasms, paralysis, and other neurological problems. If ingested, it is essential to note that the exact dosage and duration of consumption required to cause harm are not well-documented. However, it is recommended to avoid eating Lathyrus lenticula altogether, especially for extended periods. Consuming small amounts occasionally is unlikely to cause significant harm but should still be done with caution.
